title: Short title and commencement
verbatim:
(1) This Act may be cited as the Personal Data Protection Act 2010.
(2) This Act comes into operation on a date to be appointed by the Minister by notification in the Gazette, and the Minister may appoint different dates for the coming into operation of different provisions of this Act.
translation: Names the Act as the 'Personal Data Protection Act 2010' and grants the Minister power to appoint the commencement date(s) by Gazette notification. The Act came into force on 15 November 2013.

title: Interpretation
translation: Defines all key terms used throughout the Act. Critical definitions include: 'personal data' (information relating to identifiable individuals in commercial transactions), 'processing' (any operation on personal data), 'data user' (controller of personal data), 'data processor' (processor acting on behalf of data user), 'data subject' (the individual concerned), 'sensitive personal data' (health, political, religious, criminal data), 'consent' (freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous), and new terms added by the 2024 Amendment: 'data breach', 'data protection officer', 'biometric data', and 'health data'.

title: Application of Act
verbatim:
(1) This Act applies to any person who processes personal data in respect of commercial transactions.
(2) This Act applies to a data user who is established in Malaysia and processes personal data in the context of that establishment.
(3) This Act applies to a data user who is not established in Malaysia but uses equipment in Malaysia for processing personal data other than for the purposes of transit through Malaysia.
(4) Notwithstanding subsection (1), this Act shall not apply to the Federal Government and State Governments.
translation: Defines the scope of the Act. It applies to any person processing personal data in commercial transactions. It covers data users established in Malaysia and those outside Malaysia who use equipment within Malaysia for processing. Importantly, it explicitly excludes Federal and State Government bodies from the Act's application.

title: Non-application of Act
translation: Lists specific exclusions from the Act. Personal data processed for purely personal or household use is excluded. Processing solely for journalistic, literary or academic purposes is excluded where necessary to balance privacy with freedom of expression. Data processed outside Malaysia is excluded unless intended for further Malaysian processing. Licensed credit reporting businesses are also excluded.

title: Application of personal data protection principles
translation: Establishes the mandatory obligation on all data users to comply with all seven Personal Data Protection Principles when processing personal data. Lists the seven principles that form the core framework of the Act.

title: General Principle
translation: The General Principle: Personal data must not be processed without the data subject's consent. However, consent is not required when processing is necessary for contract performance, legal compliance, protecting vital interests, administration of justice, exercise of statutory functions, or legitimate interests of the data user (provided these do not override the data subject's rights).

title: Notice and Choice Principle
translation: The Notice and Choice Principle: Data users must inform data subjects, before or upon data collection, of key information including: the data user's identity, the types of data collected, the purpose of processing, disclosure information, data subject rights, whether supply is voluntary or mandatory, and whether data will be transferred overseas. Data users must also respond to enquiries about whether their data is being processed.

title: Disclosure Principle
translation: The Disclosure Principle: Personal data must not be disclosed for purposes other than those stated at collection, or to parties other than those notified, unless the data subject consents. Exceptions include disclosures for crime prevention or detection, legal proceedings, legal advice, protecting vital interests, law enforcement investigations, or legal compliance obligations.

title: Security Principle
translation: The Security Principle: Data users must implement practical steps to protect personal data against loss, misuse, modification, unauthorized access, alteration, or destruction. The appropriate level of security must consider the nature of the data, the harm potential, storage location, equipment security, staff reliability, and transfer security. Where a data processor is used, the data user must ensure a written contract exists and that the processor implements adequate security.

title: Retention Principle
verbatim:
(1) Personal data processed for any purpose shall not be kept longer than is necessary for the fulfillment of that purpose.
(2) It is the duty of a data user to take all reasonable steps to ensure that all personal data is destroyed or permanently deleted if it is no longer required for the purpose for which it was to be processed.
translation: The Retention Principle: Personal data must not be retained for longer than is necessary for the purpose for which it was collected. Data users have an active duty to destroy or permanently delete personal data once it is no longer needed for the original purpose.

title: Data Integrity Principle
verbatim: A data user shall take reasonable steps to ensure that the personal data is accurate, complete, not misleading and kept up-to-date by having regard to the purpose, including any directly related purpose, for which the personal data was collected and further processed.
translation: The Data Integrity Principle: Data users must take reasonable steps to ensure that personal data remains accurate, complete, not misleading, and up-to-date, having regard to the purposes for which it was collected and further processed.

title: Access Principle
translation: The Access Principle: Data users must allow data subjects to access their own personal data held by the data user, and to correct that data if it is inaccurate, incomplete, misleading, or not up-to-date (subject to lawful grounds for refusal).

title: Prohibition on processing of personal data without registration
verbatim:
(1) No data user in any class of data users as prescribed under this Act shall process personal data unless the data user is registered under this Part.
(2) A class of data users shall be prescribed by the Minister by order published in the Gazette.
(3) Any data user who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years; or
(c) to both.
translation: Data users in prescribed classes are prohibited from processing personal data unless registered with the Commissioner. The Minister prescribes which classes of data users must register by Gazette order. Currently prescribed industries include communications, banking, insurance, health, tourism, transport, education, direct sales, services, utilities, and real estate. Contravention is a criminal offence.

title: Application for registration
verbatim:
(1) A data user who is required to be registered under this Part shall make an application for registration to the Commissioner in the prescribed form together with the prescribed fee.
(2) The Commissioner may, upon receiving an application under subsection (1)—
(a) register the data user; or
(b) refuse to register the data user.
(3) Where the Commissioner refuses to register an applicant, the Commissioner shall notify the applicant in writing of such refusal.
(4) Where the Commissioner registers a data user, the Commissioner shall issue to the data user a certificate of registration.
(5) The Commissioner may refuse to register an applicant if the applicant—
(a) is an undischarged bankrupt or has entered into a composition with his creditors;
(b) has been convicted of any offence under this Act or any written law relating to or involving fraud, dishonesty or moral turpitude;
(c) is not a fit and proper person; or
(d) is a company that is in receivership or under judicial management.
translation: Prescribes the procedure for data users to apply for registration. Applications must be made in prescribed form with the prescribed fee. The Commissioner may register or refuse registration. Grounds for refusal include bankruptcy, criminal convictions for fraud or dishonesty, not being a fit and proper person, or the company being in receivership.

title: Renewal of registration
verbatim:
(1) A data user who is registered under this Part shall, before the expiry of his registration, apply for the renewal of registration in the prescribed form together with the prescribed fee.
(2) A registration shall be for such period as may be prescribed and may be renewed for further periods as may be prescribed.
(3) The Commissioner may renew or refuse to renew the registration of a data user.
(4) Where the Commissioner refuses to renew a registration, the Commissioner shall notify the data user in writing of the grounds for refusal.
translation: Registered data users must apply for renewal before their registration expires, in the prescribed form and with the prescribed fee. The Commissioner may renew or refuse renewal and must provide written reasons for any refusal.

title: Suspension and cancellation of registration
translation: The Commissioner has power to suspend or cancel a data user's registration on specified grounds including cessation of processing, criminal conviction, breach of conditions, false statements, or insolvency. Natural justice requires written notice and an opportunity for the data user to make representations. Even after suspension or cancellation, the data user must continue to comply with the Act for personal data already held.

title: Processing of sensitive personal data
translation: Processing of sensitive personal data (health, political opinions, religious beliefs, criminal records, and other Minister-designated categories) requires explicit consent from the data subject, or must fall within specified exceptional grounds such as employment law obligations, vital interests protection, legal proceedings, medical purposes by health professionals, or other specified purposes. This section supersedes the General Principle for sensitive data.

title: Processing for purposes of direct marketing
verbatim:
(1) A data user shall not process personal data in respect of a data subject for the purposes of direct marketing, unless—
(a) the data user has informed the data subject that the data subject has the right to object, without charge, to such processing; and
(b) the data subject has not objected to such processing.
(2) Where a data user has not complied with subsection (1)(a), a data subject may give notice in writing to the data user requiring the data user to cease, or not to begin, processing his personal data for the purposes of direct marketing.
(3) A data user who receives a notice under subsection (2) shall cease to process the data subject's personal data for the purposes of direct marketing within twenty-one days from the date of receipt of such notice.
(4) A data user who contravenes subsection (3) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding two hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
translation: Data users processing personal data for direct marketing must first inform data subjects of their right to object at no charge, and must not proceed if the data subject has objected. Data subjects may give written notice to cease direct marketing processing at any time, and the data user must comply within 21 days. Contravention is a criminal offence.

title: Processing causing damage or distress
verbatim:
(1) An individual is entitled to require a data user to cease, or not to begin, processing, or processing for a specified purpose or in a specified manner, any personal data in respect of which he is the data subject, on the ground that, for specified reasons—
(a) the processing of that personal data or the processing of that personal data for that purpose or in that manner is causing or is likely to cause substantial damage or substantial distress to the data subject or to another; and
(b) that damage or distress is or would be unwarranted.
(2) The data subject shall give notice in writing to the data user specifying the personal data concerned, the reason for the ground of the objection, and the damage or distress it is causing.
(3) A data user who receives a notice from a data subject under subsection (2) shall within twenty-one days from the date of receipt of the notice give the data subject a written notice stating either—
(a) that the data user has complied or intends to comply with the notice; or
(b) that the data user regards the notice as to any extent unjustified and the reasons for that view.
translation: Data subjects may require a data user to cease processing of their personal data where that processing is causing or is likely to cause substantial unwarranted damage or distress to them or another. Written notice must be given specifying the data, reason, and harm. The data user must respond in writing within 21 days either confirming compliance or explaining why the objection is considered unjustified.

title: Automated decision-making
verbatim:
(1) An individual is entitled at any time, by notice in writing to a data user, to require the data user to ensure that no decision taken by or on behalf of the data user which significantly affects that individual is based solely on the processing by automatic means of personal data in respect of which that individual is the data subject for the purpose of evaluating matters relating to that individual such as, for example, his performance at work, his creditworthiness, his reliability, his conduct, and so on.
(2) Where a decision which significantly affects an individual is taken by or on behalf of a data user based solely on such processing as is mentioned in subsection (1), the data user shall as soon as reasonably practicable notify the data subject that the decision was taken on that basis.
(3) Where a data user is required by subsection (2) to notify a data subject, the data subject may, within twenty-one days of receiving the notification, require the data user by notice in writing to reconsider the decision or to take a new decision on a basis that is not solely automatic.
(4) The data user shall, within twenty-one days of receiving a notice under subsection (3), give the data subject a written notice specifying the steps taken by the data user to comply with the request.
translation: Data subjects have the right to require that no decision significantly affecting them is based solely on automated processing (e.g., automated credit scoring, performance evaluation, reliability assessment). If such a decision is made, the data user must promptly notify the data subject, who then has 21 days to request human review or reconsideration. The data user must respond with steps taken within 21 days.

title: Processing of personal data by data user for his agent
verbatim:
(1) Where a data user engages a data processor to process personal data on his behalf, the data user shall ensure that the data processor processes the personal data only on instructions from the data user.
(2) A data user who engages a data processor for the processing of personal data shall ensure that—
(a) the data processor implements appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect personal data;
(b) the data processor processes personal data only on instructions from the data user; and
(c) the processing is carried out pursuant to a written agreement between the data user and the data processor.
translation: When a data user engages a data processor (third-party processor), the data user must ensure the processor acts only on the data user's instructions, implements appropriate technical and organisational security measures, and operates under a written agreement. This establishes the controller-processor relationship in Malaysian law.

title: Transfer of personal data to places outside Malaysia
translation: Cross-border data transfers are generally prohibited except to places approved by the Minister, or where specific conditions are met: data subject consent, contract necessity, substantial public interest, legal proceedings, vital interests protection, public register data, or Commissioner-approved transfer terms. The 2024 Amendment expanded the mechanisms via new section 129A.

title: Processing of personal data in relation to obtaining data
translation: As a general rule, personal data should be obtained directly from the data subject. Exceptions apply where the data subject is a minor or lacks legal competence, where the data subject has consented to third-party sourcing, for crime prevention or legal requirements, or where direct collection is not reasonably practicable.

title: Code of practice
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, after consultation with the Advisory Committee, issue codes of practice for the guidance of data users in the processing of personal data in any particular class of industry or activity.
(2) A code of practice may be issued by the Commissioner specifying the practices which, in the opinion of the Commissioner, are desirable practices to be adopted by data users for the purposes of ensuring compliance with the personal data protection principles and this Act.
(3) Contravention of a code of practice shall not of itself render a person liable to any civil or criminal proceedings, but such a code shall be admissible in any proceedings under this Act and, if any provision of such a code appears to the court or tribunal concerned to be relevant to any question arising in the proceedings, it shall be taken into account in determining that question.
translation: The Commissioner may issue industry-specific codes of practice to guide data users on compliant data processing. Contravention of a code of practice is not itself a criminal offence but codes are admissible as evidence in proceedings and will be taken into account by courts and tribunals.

title: Enforcement notice
translation: Where the Commissioner is satisfied a data user has breached any data protection principle, an enforcement notice may be issued requiring specific remedial actions within a specified time. Actions can include rectification, erasure, cessation of processing, or blocking access. Contravening an enforcement notice is a criminal offence.

title: Urgency of enforcement notice
verbatim:
(1) If the Commissioner is satisfied—
(a) that a data user has contravened or is contravening any of the data protection principles; and
(b) that the contravention is likely to cause damage or distress to any data subject if not addressed urgently,
the Commissioner may, with the approval of the Minister, include in the enforcement notice a statement indicating that the enforcement notice is to have effect immediately.
(2) An enforcement notice which includes a statement under subsection (1) shall have effect immediately upon service.
(3) An enforcement notice which does not include such a statement shall not take effect if, before the end of such period as may be specified in the notice, an appeal has been brought against the notice.
translation: In urgent cases where a data protection principle contravention is likely to cause imminent damage or distress, the Commissioner may with Ministerial approval issue an enforcement notice that takes immediate effect upon service, rather than waiting for any appeal period to expire.

title: Request for assessment
verbatim:
(1) A person who is, or believes himself to be, directly affected by the processing of personal data may request the Commissioner to make an assessment of whether the processing has been or is being carried out in compliance with the provisions of this Act.
(2) On receiving a request under this section, the Commissioner shall make an assessment in such manner as appears to him to be appropriate, unless the Commissioner has not been supplied with sufficient information to enable him to identify the person making the request and to identify the processing in question.
(3) The matters to which the Commissioner may have regard in deciding how to act on a request include—
(a) the extent to which the request appears to raise a matter of substance;
(b) any undue delay in making the request; and
(c) whether or not the person making the request is entitled to make an access request in relation to the personal data in question.
(4) Subject to subsection (5), the Commissioner shall notify the person who made the request of any assessment he has made in response to the request.
(5) The Commissioner shall not disclose any information obtained in the course of making an assessment if, in his opinion, the disclosure would not be in the public interest.
translation: Any person who believes they are directly affected by personal data processing may request the Commissioner to assess whether processing complies with the Act. The Commissioner must assess the request (unless identifying information is insufficient) and notify the requester of the assessment outcome, subject to public interest limitations on information disclosure.

title: Appeal against enforcement notice
verbatim:
(1) A data user who is served with an enforcement notice may appeal to the Appeal Tribunal against the notice within thirty days of the date of service of the notice.
(2) On an appeal under this section, the Tribunal may—
(a) allow the appeal and quash the enforcement notice;
(b) vary the notice in any way; or
(c) dismiss the appeal and confirm the enforcement notice.
(3) Pending the determination of an appeal, the enforcement notice shall not take effect unless the notice contains a statement that the Commissioner considers that the notice should have immediate effect by reason of urgency.
translation: A data user may appeal an enforcement notice to the Appeal Tribunal within 30 days. The Tribunal may quash, vary, or confirm the notice. Pending appeal, the enforcement notice does not take effect unless it contains an urgency statement made by the Commissioner.

title: Failure to comply with enforcement notice
verbatim:
A data user who contravenes an enforcement notice commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years; or
(c) to both.
translation: Non-compliance with an enforcement notice is a criminal offence punishable by a fine of up to RM300,000, imprisonment of up to two years, or both.

title: Right of access to personal data
verbatim:
(1) Subject to this Act, an individual is entitled to be informed by a data user whether the data user processes personal data of which the individual is the data subject.
(2) An individual who is informed under subsection (1) that personal data of which he is the data subject is being processed by or on behalf of the data user is entitled, on making a request in writing to the data user—
(a) to be supplied by the data user with a written description of the personal data held, the purposes for which it is being or is to be processed and a description of any third party or class of third party to whom it is or may be disclosed;
(b) to have communicated to him in an intelligible form the information constituting any such personal data and any information available to the data user as to the source of that data;
(c) to know the logic involved in any automatic processing constituting or likely to constitute the sole basis for any decision significantly affecting that individual.
(3) A data user shall respond to a request for access within twenty-one days of receipt.
(4) A data user may charge a fee, not exceeding the prescribed amount, for complying with a request for access.
(5) Where the supply of information under this section would identify another individual who can be identified from the information, the data user is not obliged to supply the information unless that other individual has consented to the disclosure.
translation: Data subjects have the right to request confirmation from a data user about whether their personal data is being processed. Upon written request, data subjects are entitled to: a written description of the data held, the purposes and recipients; the data in intelligible form with source information; and the logic behind any automated decision-making significantly affecting them. The data user must respond within 21 days and may charge a prescribed fee.

title: Exemptions from right of access
verbatim:
(1) The right of access under section 30 does not apply to personal data if the disclosure of the data would reveal information about another individual who can be identified from that information and that other individual has not consented to the disclosure.
(2) Personal data is exempt from the access right if the data consists of information in respect of which a claim to legal professional privilege could be maintained in legal proceedings.
(3) Personal data is exempt from the access right where the personal data is held for the purpose of preventing or detecting crime, the apprehension or prosecution of offenders, or the assessment or collection of any tax, duty, impost or levy, and the application of those rights in any particular case would be likely to prejudice any of the matters mentioned in this subsection.
(4) The Minister may by order prescribe categories of personal data that are exempt from the right of access.
translation: Lists grounds for exemption from the right of access, including: data that would reveal another unidentified individual's information, legally privileged information, data held for crime prevention, law enforcement, or tax collection purposes where access would prejudice those purposes, and other categories prescribed by the Minister.

title: Rectification, blocking, erasure and destruction of personal data
verbatim:
(1) If a court is satisfied on the application of a data subject that personal data in respect of which the applicant is the data subject is inaccurate, the court may order the data user to rectify, block, erase or destroy those data and any other personal data in respect of the applicant which contains an expression of opinion which appears to the court to be based on the inaccurate data.
(2) Subsection (1) applies whether or not the data accurately records information received or obtained by the data user from the data subject or a third party but where the data accurately records such information, the court shall not make an order under subsection (1) unless it is satisfied—
(a) that the data subject has taken reasonable steps to notify the data user of the inaccuracy; and
(b) the data user has failed within a reasonable time to comply with a request from the data subject to rectify, block, erase or destroy the data.
(3) If the court is satisfied under subsection (2) that the data accurately records information received, it may, instead of making an order specified therein, make an order requiring the data to be supplemented by such statement of true facts relating to the matters dealt with by the data as the court may approve.
(4) Where the court—
(a) makes an order under subsection (1); or
(b) is satisfied that personal data which have been rectified, blocked, erased or destroyed were inaccurate,
it may, where it considers it reasonably practicable having regard to the number of persons involved and the nature of the case, order the data user to notify third parties to whom the data has been disclosed of the rectification, blocking, erasure or destruction.
translation: Data subjects may apply to court for an order requiring a data user to rectify, block, erase, or destroy inaccurate personal data (including opinions based on inaccurate data). Where data accurately recorded information received, the court may instead order a supplementary statement of true facts. The court may also order notification to third parties who received the inaccurate data.

title: Right to prevent processing likely to cause damage or distress
verbatim:
An individual is entitled at any time by notice in writing to a data user to require the data user at the end of such period as is reasonable in the circumstances to cease, or not to begin, processing, or processing for a specified purpose or in a specified manner, any personal data in respect of which he is the data subject, on the ground that, for specified reasons—
(a) the processing of that personal data or the processing of that personal data for that purpose or in that manner is causing or is likely to cause substantial damage or substantial distress to the data subject or to another; and
(b) that damage or distress is or would be unwarranted.
translation: Data subjects may serve written notice on data users to cease processing of their personal data where such processing is causing or is likely to cause substantial unwarranted damage or distress to themselves or another person.

title: Compliance with notice under section 33
verbatim:
(1) A data user who has been required under section 33 by a data subject to cease processing personal data shall within twenty-one days of the date of receipt of the notice, give the data subject a written notice stating—
(a) that the data user has complied or intends to comply with the request; or
(b) that the data user regards the notice as to any extent unjustified and the reasons for that view.
(2) If the data user notifies the data subject of the data user's refusal to comply, the data subject may seek the assistance of the Commissioner who may assess and investigate the matter and take such action as the Commissioner considers appropriate.
translation: A data user must respond to a section 33 notice within 21 days, either confirming compliance or explaining why the notice is unjustified. Where a data user refuses, the data subject may seek the Commissioner's assistance to investigate and take appropriate action.

title: Rights in relation to automated decision-making
verbatim:
(1) An individual is entitled at any time, by notice in writing to a data user, to require the data user to ensure that no decision taken by or on behalf of the data user which significantly affects that individual is based solely on the processing by automatic means of personal data in respect of which that individual is the data subject.
(2) Where a decision that significantly affects a data subject is based solely on the processing by automatic means of personal data, the data user shall—
(a) notify the data subject that the decision was taken on that basis as soon as reasonably practicable; and
(b) allow the data subject to make representations within twenty-one days.
(3) A data user who receives representations from a data subject under subsection (2)(b) shall consider those representations and take such actions as the data user considers appropriate.
translation: Data subjects may object in writing to decisions significantly affecting them that are based solely on automated processing. Where such an automated decision has been made, the data user must notify the data subject promptly and allow 21 days for representations. The data user must consider any representations received and take appropriate action.

title: Compensation for failure to comply with requirements
verbatim:
(1) An individual who suffers damage by reason of any contravention by a data user of any requirement of this Act is entitled to compensation from the data user for that damage.
(2) In proceedings brought against a data user by virtue of this section, it is a defence to prove that the data user had taken such care as in all the circumstances was reasonably required to comply with the requirement concerned.
(3) The court may, in addition to awarding compensation under this section, make an order requiring the data user to take the steps specified in the order to comply with the requirements of this Act.
(4) For the purposes of this section, 'damage' includes any distress suffered by the data subject.
translation: Data subjects who suffer damage (including distress) due to a data user's contravention of the Act are entitled to compensation. Data users have a defence if they can prove they took all reasonably required care to comply. Courts may also order remedial steps in addition to compensation.

title: Rectification, blocking, erasure and destruction
verbatim:
(1) A data subject may request a data user in writing to correct personal data that is inaccurate, incomplete, misleading or not up-to-date.
(2) The data user shall, within twenty-one days of receipt of the request—
(a) correct the personal data and provide the data subject with written confirmation of the correction; or
(b) if the data user believes the data is accurate, notify the data subject in writing of the reasons for not correcting the data.
(3) Where a data user is unable to agree with the data subject on the accuracy of the personal data, the data user shall attach to the data a statement from the data subject of the corrections sought.
(4) Where personal data has been corrected under this section, the data user shall, within twenty-one days, notify any person to whom the personal data was disclosed during the twelve months preceding the date of correction of the correction, and that person shall correct the personal data accordingly.
translation: Data subjects may request correction of inaccurate, incomplete, misleading, or outdated personal data. The data user must respond within 21 days either making the correction or providing written reasons for refusal. Where accuracy is disputed, a statement of correction must be attached to the data. When data is corrected, third parties who received the data within the preceding 12 months must also be notified within 21 days.

title: Refusal of request for correction
verbatim:
(1) A data user may refuse a request for correction from a data subject if the data user is satisfied that—
(a) the personal data is accurate;
(b) the data subject has not provided sufficient proof of inaccuracy;
(c) the correction would be contrary to any written law; or
(d) the correction would violate the interests of another person.
(2) Where a data user refuses a request for correction, the data user shall notify the data subject in writing of the refusal and the reasons.
(3) A data subject who is refused a correction may seek the Commissioner's assistance.
translation: A data user may refuse a correction request if satisfied the data is accurate, insufficient proof of inaccuracy was provided, correction would breach written law, or it would violate another person's interests. Written reasons for refusal must be provided. Refused data subjects may seek the Commissioner's assistance.

title: Fees for access request
verbatim:
(1) A data user may charge a fee for compliance with an access request.
(2) The fee charged under subsection (1) shall not exceed the prescribed amount.
(3) A data user is not obliged to comply with an access request unless it is accompanied by the applicable fee.
translation: Data users may charge a fee for complying with access requests, subject to the prescribed maximum fee. Data users are not obliged to comply with an access request unless accompanied by the applicable fee.

title: Processing of personal data of children
verbatim:
(1) No data user shall process personal data relating to a child unless the consent of the parent, guardian or person with lawful custody of the child is obtained.
(2) A 'child' for the purposes of this section means a person under the age of eighteen years.
(3) Where the data user knows, or ought reasonably to know, that the data subject is a child, the data user must take reasonable steps to verify that the consent required under subsection (1) has been obtained from a parent or guardian.
(4) Contravention of this section constitutes an offence and a person convicted thereof shall be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or
(c) to both.
translation: Processing personal data of children (persons under 18) requires parental or guardian consent. Data users who know or reasonably ought to know the data subject is a child must verify that parental consent has been obtained. This is a criminal offence with enhanced penalties of up to RM500,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment.

title: Right to data portability
verbatim:
(1) A data subject may request a data user to provide the personal data of the data subject in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format.
(2) A data user who receives a request under subsection (1) shall provide the personal data in the format requested or in a standard machine-readable format approved by the Commissioner.
(3) The data user shall comply with a request under this section within twenty-one days of receipt of the request.
(4) Where technically feasible, the data subject may request that the personal data be transmitted directly to another data user.
(5) The right of portability under this section applies to personal data that—
(a) the data subject has provided to the data user;
(b) is processed by automated means; and
(c) is processed on the basis of consent or pursuant to a contract with the data subject.
(6) The right of portability shall not adversely affect the rights and freedoms of others.
translation: Introduced by the 2024 Amendment, data subjects have the right to receive their personal data from a data user in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format, and where technically feasible, to request direct transmission to another data user. The right applies to data provided by the data subject, processed automatically, on the basis of consent or contract. The data user must comply within 21 days.

title: Right to withdraw consent
verbatim:
(1) A data subject may at any time withdraw his consent for the processing of personal data by giving written notice to the data user.
(2) Where consent is withdrawn under subsection (1)—
(a) the data user shall cease to process the personal data upon receipt of the notice, unless there are compelling legitimate grounds for the processing to continue; and
(b) the withdrawal of consent shall not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent prior to its withdrawal.
(3) The data user shall inform the data subject, prior to giving consent, of the consequences of withdrawing consent.
(4) The withdrawal of consent shall be as easy as giving consent.
translation: Data subjects may withdraw consent for processing at any time by written notice. Data users must cease processing upon receiving withdrawal notice, unless there are compelling legitimate grounds for continuation. Withdrawal does not affect the lawfulness of prior processing. The process of withdrawing consent must be as easy as giving it, and data subjects must be informed in advance of the consequences of withdrawal.

title: Right to erasure
translation: Introduced by the 2024 Amendment, data subjects have a right to erasure ('right to be forgotten') where data is no longer necessary, consent is withdrawn, the data subject objects, data has been unlawfully processed, or legal compliance requires it. Data users must erase without undue delay unless retention is legally required. Where data was made public, the data user must take reasonable steps to notify other processors. The right does not apply to freedom of expression, information, or legal compliance processing.

title: Right to restrict processing
verbatim:
(1) A data subject has the right to obtain from the data user restriction of processing where—
(a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the data user to verify the accuracy;
(b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes erasure and requests restriction instead;
(c) the data user no longer needs the personal data for the purposes of processing but it is required by the data subject for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; or
(d) the data subject has objected to processing pending verification of whether the legitimate grounds of the data user override those of the data subject.
(2) Where processing has been restricted, such personal data shall, with the exception of storage, only be processed with the data subject's consent or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims, or for the protection of the rights of another natural or legal person, or for reasons of important public interest.
(3) A data user shall inform a data subject who has obtained restriction of processing before the restriction is lifted.
translation: Introduced by the 2024 Amendment, data subjects have the right to restrict processing (limiting it to storage only) while accuracy is disputed, while processing legitimacy is assessed, when data is needed for legal claims despite no longer being needed by the data user, or while an objection is being assessed. Restricted data can only be processed with consent, for legal claims, to protect others, or for public interest. The data user must inform the data subject before lifting a restriction.

title: Exemptions
verbatim:
(1) The Minister may by order published in the Gazette exempt any data user or any class of data users from all or any of the provisions of this Act.
(2) An exemption under subsection (1) may be given subject to conditions.
(3) An exemption may be withdrawn by the Minister by order published in the Gazette if the data user breaches any condition imposed under subsection (2) or if the Minister considers it appropriate to do so.
(4) Any data user who processes personal data in reliance on an exemption under this section and who breaches any condition imposed in respect of that exemption commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years; or
(c) to both.
translation: The Minister has broad power to exempt any data user or class of data users from all or any provisions of the Act, by Gazette order. Exemptions may be conditional and may be withdrawn. Breaching exemption conditions is a criminal offence. This ministerial power allows tailored regulatory flexibility.

title: Exemption for personal data processed outside Malaysia
verbatim:
(1) Personal data processed outside Malaysia is exempt from this Act unless it is intended for further processing in Malaysia.
(2) Personal data that is in transit through Malaysia and is not intended for further processing in Malaysia is exempt from this Act.
(3) For the purposes of subsection (1), 'further processing' includes any processing in Malaysia following the transfer of personal data from outside Malaysia, including the use, disclosure, storage, or organisation of such data within Malaysia.
translation: Personal data processed entirely outside Malaysia is exempt from the Act unless it is intended to be further processed within Malaysia. Data that is merely in transit through Malaysia is also exempt. The definition of 'further processing' is broad and includes any use, disclosure, storage or organisation within Malaysia following an international transfer.

title: Appointment of Commissioner
verbatim:
(1) The Minister shall appoint a person to be the Personal Data Protection Commissioner.
(2) The Commissioner shall be appointed on such terms and conditions as the Minister may determine.
(3) The Commissioner shall hold office for a term not exceeding three years and shall be eligible for reappointment.
(4) The Commissioner may resign from office by giving not less than one month's written notice to the Minister.
(5) The Minister may terminate the appointment of the Commissioner if the Commissioner—
(a) is unable to perform his functions;
(b) is declared a bankrupt;
(c) is convicted of an offence;
(d) is guilty of misconduct; or
(e) has contravened any of the terms and conditions of his appointment.
(6) The Commissioner shall not hold any other office of profit while holding office as Commissioner.
translation: Establishes the office of Personal Data Protection Commissioner appointed by the Minister for terms up to 3 years (renewable). The Commissioner must not hold other paid positions. Grounds for termination include inability to perform duties, bankruptcy, criminal conviction, misconduct, or breach of appointment terms.

title: Functions of Commissioner
translation: Sets out the broad functions of the Commissioner including: exercising statutory powers, advising the Minister, public education, issuing codes of practice, research, registration of data users, investigating complaints, maintaining the data user register, international co-operation, and annual reporting to Parliament.

title: Powers of Commissioner
translation: Confers powers on the Commissioner including delegation to officers, requiring information from persons, access to equipment, issuing enforcement notices, carrying out audits and inspections, and applying to court for compliance orders.

title: Register of data users
translation: The Commissioner must maintain a public register of all registered data users. The register must include the data user's details, descriptions of data processed, data subjects, processing purposes, intended disclosures, and cross-border transfer intentions. The register is freely available to the public both in person and online.

title: Notification of changes
verbatim:
(1) A registered data user shall notify the Commissioner of any change in the information submitted to the Commissioner for the purposes of registration within thirty days of the date the change takes effect.
(2) A data user who fails to comply with subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit.
translation: Registered data users must notify the Commissioner of any changes to their registration information within 30 days of the change taking effect. Failure to notify is a criminal offence with a fine of up to RM100,000.

title: Audit
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may at any time carry out an audit of the personal data processing activities of any data user to assess whether the data user is complying with the provisions of this Act.
(2) The Commissioner shall give reasonable notice to the data user before conducting an audit, except where the Commissioner considers that giving notice would defeat the purpose of the audit.
(3) A data user shall—
(a) permit the Commissioner or any person authorised by the Commissioner to have access to the data user's premises, equipment, books, documents and other records;
(b) provide reasonable assistance to the Commissioner; and
(c) not obstruct the Commissioner in the conduct of the audit.
(4) A data user who contravenes subsection (3)(c) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
translation: The Commissioner has power to audit data users' personal data processing activities at any time. Reasonable notice is generally required, except where it would defeat the audit's purpose. Data users must cooperate, provide access and assistance, and must not obstruct the Commissioner. Obstruction is a criminal offence.

title: Requirement to provide information
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, by written notice served on any person, require that person to provide to the Commissioner, within such period (not less than seven days) as may be specified in the notice, information relevant to the discharge of the Commissioner's functions.
(2) The Commissioner may require information under this section in relation to compliance with the Act, processing of personal data, registration matters, data breaches, or any other matter relevant to the Commissioner's functions.
(3) A person who fails to comply with a notice under subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit.
(4) A person who provides false or misleading information in response to a notice under this section commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years; or
(c) to both.
translation: The Commissioner may require any person to provide information (with at least 7 days notice) relevant to the Commissioner's functions. This covers compliance, processing activities, registration, data breaches and other relevant matters. Failure to provide information is an offence (RM100,000 fine). Providing false or misleading information is a more serious offence (RM300,000 fine and/or 2 years imprisonment).

title: Commissioner may seek information from other authorities
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, for the purpose of performing his functions under this Act, seek information from, and share information with, any government authority, regulatory body, or enforcement agency, whether within or outside Malaysia.
(2) Any government authority, regulatory body or enforcement agency may provide information to the Commissioner upon request.
(3) The Commissioner shall not disclose any information obtained under this section unless the disclosure is—
(a) necessary for the exercise of the Commissioner's functions;
(b) required by law; or
(c) made with the consent of the person from whom the information was obtained.
translation: The Commissioner may share and seek information with government authorities, regulators, and enforcement agencies domestically and internationally. Information obtained through this channel is subject to confidentiality and can only be disclosed for performing the Commissioner's functions, legal requirements, or with the provider's consent.

title: Engagement of professional advice
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may engage such persons as he thinks fit to assist in the performance of the Commissioner's functions.
(2) Any person engaged under subsection (1) shall be subject to the direction and control of the Commissioner.
(3) The remuneration and expenses of persons engaged under this section shall be paid out of the Personal Data Protection Fund.
translation: The Commissioner may engage external advisors, consultants, and other persons to assist with the Commissioner's functions. Such persons are under the Commissioner's control and are remunerated from the Personal Data Protection Fund.

title: Protection of Commissioner and staff
verbatim:
(1) No action, suit, prosecution, or other proceedings shall be brought, instituted or maintained in any court or tribunal against—
(a) the Commissioner; or
(b) any officer, employee, or agent of the Commissioner,
for or on account of or in respect of any act, matter or thing done or omitted to be done in good faith in the exercise or purported exercise of any power, or in the performance or purported performance of any function conferred upon the Commissioner or such officer, employee or agent, as the case may be, by this Act.
translation: Grants statutory immunity to the Commissioner and staff from legal proceedings for acts done in good faith in the exercise of their statutory powers and functions. This immunity does not cover bad faith actions.

title: Secrecy
verbatim:
(1) Except for the purpose of the performance of his duties or the exercise of his functions under this Act or when lawfully required to do so by a court or under any written law, the Commissioner and every officer, employee or agent of the Commissioner shall not—
(a) disclose or communicate to any person any information, document, or matter which may come to his knowledge in the course of his duties; or
(b) permit any person to have access to any records in the possession of the Commissioner.
(2) Any person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years; or
(c) to both.
translation: The Commissioner and all staff are bound by strict secrecy obligations. They must not disclose information obtained in the course of their duties except for performing statutory functions, complying with court orders, or meeting legal requirements. Breach of secrecy is a criminal offence.

title: Annual report
translation: The Commissioner must prepare an annual report for the Minister covering complaints, investigations, enforcement actions, data breach statistics, codes of practice, registration activities, and law reform recommendations. The Minister must table this report before Parliament.

title: Deputy Commissioner
verbatim:
(1) The Minister may appoint one or more Deputy Commissioners to assist the Commissioner.
(2) The Deputy Commissioner shall perform such functions and exercise such powers as may be assigned to him by the Commissioner.
(3) In the absence of the Commissioner or where the office of Commissioner is vacant, the Deputy Commissioner may perform all the functions and exercise all the powers of the Commissioner.
(4) The provisions of this Act relating to the Commissioner, including sections 56 and 57, shall apply to the Deputy Commissioner.
translation: The Minister may appoint one or more Deputy Commissioners to assist the Commissioner. The Deputy Commissioner performs functions assigned by the Commissioner and may act as Commissioner in the Commissioner's absence. The same protections, immunities, and secrecy obligations that apply to the Commissioner also apply to the Deputy Commissioner.

title: Officers and staff of Commissioner
verbatim:
(1) There shall be employed such officers and staff as may be necessary to assist the Commissioner in the performance of his functions.
(2) The Commissioner may, subject to the approval of the Minister, determine the terms and conditions of service of officers and staff employed under this section.
(3) Officers and staff employed under this section shall be subject to the secrecy provisions in section 57.
(4) The Commissioner may designate any officer as an authorised officer for the purposes of carrying out inspections, investigations, and other enforcement activities under this Act.
translation: Provides for the appointment of staff to assist the Commissioner, with terms and conditions determined by the Commissioner subject to Ministerial approval. All staff are bound by secrecy obligations. The Commissioner may designate specific officers as 'authorised officers' for enforcement activities.

title: Establishment of Fund
translation: Establishes the Personal Data Protection Fund under the Commissioner's control. The Fund is funded by Parliamentary appropriations, fees, fines and penalties collected under the Act, donations, investment returns, and other lawfully received moneys.

title: Application of Fund
translation: Specifies the permitted uses of the Personal Data Protection Fund including remuneration of the Commissioner and staff, Advisory Committee and Appeal Tribunal expenses, audit and investigation costs, public education campaigns, research, and international conferences and training.

title: Investment of Fund
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, with the approval of the Minister of Finance, invest any moneys of the Fund which are not immediately required for the purposes of the Fund.
(2) Any investment made under this section shall be in accordance with the Trustee Act 1949 [Act 208].
translation: The Commissioner may invest surplus Fund moneys (not immediately needed) with the Finance Minister's approval. All investments must comply with the Trustee Act 1949.

title: Financial year
verbatim: The financial year of the Fund shall be the period of twelve months commencing on 1 January of each year, except for the first financial year which shall commence on the date of establishment of the Fund and end on 31 December of the same year.
translation: Establishes the financial year of the Personal Data Protection Fund as the calendar year (1 January to 31 December), with a shortened first financial year commencing on the establishment date of the Fund.

title: Accounts
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner shall maintain proper accounts and other records in respect of the Fund.
(2) The Commissioner shall, within three months after the end of each financial year, prepare a statement of accounts in respect of that financial year.
(3) The statement of accounts shall be audited by the Auditor General or by an auditor appointed by the Commissioner with the approval of the Auditor General.
(4) The Commissioner shall, within six months after the end of each financial year, cause the audited statement of accounts to be published in the Gazette and made available to the public.
translation: The Commissioner must maintain proper accounts of the Fund and prepare annual financial statements within 3 months of the financial year end. Accounts must be audited by the Auditor General or an approved auditor. Audited accounts must be gazetted and made publicly available within 6 months of the year end.

title: Estimates of revenue and expenditure
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner shall, before the commencement of each financial year, prepare estimates of revenue and expenditure of the Fund for the coming financial year.
(2) The estimates shall be submitted to the Minister for approval before the commencement of the financial year to which they relate.
(3) The Commissioner shall not incur any expenditure in excess of the amounts specified in the approved estimates without the prior approval of the Minister.
translation: The Commissioner must prepare and submit annual revenue and expenditure estimates to the Minister for approval before the start of each financial year. No expenditure in excess of approved estimates may be incurred without prior Ministerial approval.

title: Fees to be paid into Fund
verbatim:
(1) All fees paid to the Commissioner under this Act shall be paid into the Fund.
(2) All fines and financial penalties imposed under this Act and collected by the Commissioner shall be paid into the Fund.
(3) The Commissioner may waive or reduce any fee payable under this Act in deserving cases.
translation: All fees collected under the Act (including registration fees and access request fees) and all fines and financial penalties must be paid into the Personal Data Protection Fund. The Commissioner has discretion to waive or reduce fees in appropriate cases.

title: Grants and donations
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, with the approval of the Minister, receive grants, donations, or contributions for the purposes of the Fund from any person, whether within or outside Malaysia.
(2) Any gift, donation, or contribution received under this section shall be paid into the Fund and applied in accordance with the terms and conditions (if any) subject to which the gift, donation, or contribution was made.
translation: The Commissioner may receive grants, donations, and contributions from any source (domestic or international) with Ministerial approval. Such funds must be paid into the Fund and used according to any conditions attached to the donation.

title: Surpluses
verbatim:
(1) Any surplus of the Fund after provision for all expenditures, liabilities, and obligations of the Commissioner shall be dealt with in such manner as the Minister may direct.
(2) The Minister may direct that any surplus be applied toward the purposes of the Fund, carried forward to the next financial year, or paid into the Consolidated Fund.
translation: Surplus funds remaining after all expenses and liabilities are met shall be dealt with as directed by the Minister. The Minister may direct that surpluses be reinvested in the Fund's purposes, carried forward, or transferred to the government's Consolidated Fund.

title: Establishment of Advisory Committee
translation: Establishes the Personal Data Protection Advisory Committee consisting of a Minister-appointed Chairman, the Commissioner (ex officio), and 3 to 12 other Minister-appointed members with relevant expertise in personal data, IT, law, or consumer protection.

title: Functions of Advisory Committee
translation: The Advisory Committee advises the Commissioner and Minister on personal data protection matters, reviews proposed codes of practice, advises on registration requirements for data user classes, advises on cross-border data transfer country designations, and reports annually to the Minister.

title: Meetings of Advisory Committee
verbatim:
(1) The Advisory Committee shall meet at such times and places as the Chairman may determine but shall meet at least twice in every year.
(2) The Chairman shall preside at every meeting of the Advisory Committee at which he is present.
(3) In the absence of the Chairman, the members present shall elect one of their number to preside at the meeting.
(4) The quorum for a meeting of the Advisory Committee shall be a majority of its members for the time being.
(5) All questions at a meeting of the Advisory Committee shall be decided by a majority of the votes of the members present and voting, and in the case of an equality of votes, the Chairman or the member presiding shall have a casting vote in addition to his original vote.
(6) The Advisory Committee may invite any person to attend a meeting and take part in deliberations, but that person shall have no right to vote.
translation: The Advisory Committee must meet at least twice a year, with the Chairman presiding. Quorum requires a majority of members. Decisions are by simple majority with the Chairman having a casting vote. Non-member experts may be invited to attend and contribute but cannot vote.

title: Appointment and tenure of members
translation: Advisory Committee members serve for up to 2 year terms (renewable). Members may resign with 1 month's notice. The Minister may revoke appointments for inability to perform, bankruptcy, criminal conviction, misconduct, or breach of secrecy. No compensation is payable on termination.

title: Remuneration of members
verbatim:
(1) The members of the Advisory Committee shall be paid such fees and allowances as the Minister may determine.
(2) Such fees and allowances shall be paid from the Fund.
translation: Advisory Committee members are paid fees and allowances as determined by the Minister. These are paid from the Personal Data Protection Fund.

title: Disclosure of interests
verbatim:
(1) A member of the Advisory Committee who has any direct or indirect interest in any matter being deliberated by the Advisory Committee shall, as soon as reasonably practicable after the relevant facts have come to his knowledge, disclose the nature of his interest at a meeting of the Advisory Committee.
(2) A disclosure under subsection (1) shall be recorded in the minutes of the meeting.
(3) A member who has disclosed an interest shall not take part in any deliberation or decision of the Advisory Committee with respect to that matter unless the Advisory Committee otherwise determines.
(4) A member who fails to comply with this section commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding fifty thousand ringgit.
translation: Advisory Committee members must disclose any direct or indirect interest in matters being deliberated. Disclosures are recorded in minutes. Conflicted members cannot participate in related decisions (unless the Committee determines otherwise). Failure to disclose is a criminal offence.

title: Secrecy
verbatim:
(1) Every member of the Advisory Committee and every person who attends a meeting of the Advisory Committee shall not disclose any information, matter, or thing which comes to his knowledge in the exercise of his functions or duties as a member of, or in attending, the Advisory Committee.
(2) Subsection (1) shall not apply to—
(a) the disclosure of information to the extent necessary for the performance of the functions of the Advisory Committee;
(b) the disclosure of information required by law; or
(c) the disclosure of information with the approval of the Chairman.
(3) Any person who contravenes subsection (1) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
translation: All members and attendees of Advisory Committee meetings are bound by strict confidentiality regarding information arising from their roles. Exceptions apply for performing the Committee's functions, legal requirements, or Chairman-approved disclosures. Breach is a criminal offence.

title: Committees of Advisory Committee
verbatim:
(1) The Advisory Committee may establish such committees as it thinks fit for any purpose and may delegate any of its functions to such committees.
(2) Each such committee shall consist of such members of the Advisory Committee as the Advisory Committee may determine and may include persons who are not members of the Advisory Committee.
(3) A committee established under this section shall be subject to the direction and control of the Advisory Committee.
translation: The Advisory Committee may establish sub-committees and delegate functions to them. Sub-committees may include non-Advisory-Committee members and operate under the Advisory Committee's direction.

title: Protection of members
verbatim: No action, suit, prosecution or other proceedings shall be brought, instituted or maintained in any court or tribunal against any member of the Advisory Committee for or on account of or in respect of any act, matter or thing done or omitted to be done in good faith in the exercise or purported exercise of any power, or in the performance or purported performance of any function conferred upon the member by this Act.
translation: Advisory Committee members enjoy statutory immunity from legal proceedings for acts done in good faith in the exercise of their statutory functions. This immunity is limited to good faith actions.

title: Minutes
translation: The Advisory Committee must keep minutes of all meetings, recording attendees, matters deliberated, decisions made, and interest disclosures. Confirmed and signed minutes are conclusive evidence of their contents.

title: Validity of proceedings
verbatim:
No act done or proceeding taken by the Advisory Committee shall be questioned on account of—
(a) any vacancy in the membership of the Advisory Committee; or
(b) any defect in the appointment of any member of the Advisory Committee.
translation: The validity of Advisory Committee decisions and proceedings is not affected by any vacancy in membership or any defect in a member's appointment.

title: Power to make regulations
translation: The Minister has broad power to make regulations governing the Advisory Committee's meeting procedures, conduct, and other matters necessary for its effective operation.

title: Annual report of Advisory Committee
verbatim:
(1) The Advisory Committee shall, as soon as practicable after the end of each year, submit a report to the Minister on its activities during that year.
(2) The report shall include details of matters on which advice was given, matters considered, and any recommendations made.
(3) The Minister shall cause the report to be tabled before Parliament.
translation: The Advisory Committee must submit an annual report to the Minister detailing its activities, advice given, matters considered, and recommendations. The Minister must table the report before Parliament.

title: Establishment of Appeal Tribunal
verbatim:
(1) There is established a tribunal to be known as the Personal Data Protection Appeal Tribunal.
(2) The Tribunal shall consist of—
(a) a President, who shall be a person qualified to be a judge of the High Court;
(b) two other members, who shall be persons with knowledge and experience in information technology, law, commerce or industry.
(3) The President and members shall be appointed by the Minister.
translation: Establishes the Personal Data Protection Appeal Tribunal consisting of a President (who must be qualified as a High Court judge) and two other members with expertise in IT, law, commerce or industry. All are appointed by the Minister.

title: Tenure of office of President and members
translation: The President and members serve for up to 3 year renewable terms. They may resign with 1 month's notice. The Minister may terminate appointments for inability to perform, bankruptcy, criminal conviction, misconduct, or breach of appointment terms.

title: Remuneration of President and members
verbatim:
(1) The President and members of the Tribunal shall be paid such fees and allowances as the Minister may determine.
(2) The fees and allowances shall be paid from the Fund.
translation: The President and members of the Appeal Tribunal receive fees and allowances determined by the Minister, paid from the Personal Data Protection Fund.

title: Jurisdiction of Tribunal
verbatim:
(1) The Tribunal shall have jurisdiction to hear and determine any appeal made under this Act.
(2) The Tribunal shall have the same powers as the High Court in relation to the enforcement of its orders and may enforce its orders by proceedings in the High Court as if they were orders of that Court.
(3) The Tribunal may in its discretion hear an appeal in whole or in part in private.
translation: The Appeal Tribunal has jurisdiction to hear all statutory appeals under the Act. It has the same enforcement powers as the High Court and can enforce its orders through High Court proceedings. The Tribunal may hold private hearings at its discretion.

title: Appeals to Tribunal
translation: Persons aggrieved by Commissioner decisions (including refusal of registration, cancellation/suspension of registration, and enforcement notices) may appeal to the Tribunal within 30 days of notification. Appeals must be in the prescribed form and accompanied by the prescribed fee.

title: Hearing of appeals
verbatim:
(1) The Tribunal shall, on receipt of an appeal, fix a date for the hearing of the appeal and notify the parties accordingly.
(2) The Commissioner shall be a party to every appeal.
(3) The Tribunal shall give all parties a reasonable opportunity to be heard.
(4) A party to an appeal may appear in person or be represented by an advocate and solicitor.
(5) The Tribunal may receive in evidence any statement, document, information, or matter that may in its opinion assist the Tribunal in dealing with the matter before it, whether or not the same would be admissible as evidence in a court of law.
(6) The Tribunal shall, as far as practicable, deal with an appeal within six months of it being brought.
translation: The Tribunal fixes hearing dates and notifies parties. The Commissioner is always a party to appeals. All parties have a right to be heard, may be represented by a lawyer, and the Tribunal may admit evidence not normally admissible in court. Appeals should be resolved within 6 months.

title: Decisions of Tribunal
verbatim:
(1) The Tribunal, on hearing an appeal, may—
(a) confirm, vary or reverse the decision of the Commissioner;
(b) remit the matter to the Commissioner with directions; or
(c) make such other order as it thinks fit.
(2) The Tribunal shall give reasons for its decision.
(3) A decision of the Tribunal shall be by majority.
(4) The Tribunal shall notify the parties of its decision and the reasons for the decision.
(5) A decision of the Tribunal shall be final and binding on the parties, subject to an appeal on a question of law to the High Court.
translation: The Tribunal may confirm, vary, reverse Commissioner decisions or remit with directions. Decisions require reasons, are made by majority, and are final and binding — but may be further appealed to the High Court on questions of law.

title: Stay of enforcement notice pending appeal
verbatim:
(1) The bringing of an appeal against an enforcement notice shall have the effect of suspending the notice pending the determination of the appeal, unless the enforcement notice contains a statement under section 26 indicating that the notice has immediate effect.
(2) On an application to the Tribunal, the Tribunal may in its discretion grant a stay of the enforcement of an enforcement notice pending the appeal.
(3) Where an enforcement notice contains a statement of immediate effect, the Tribunal may discharge the notice or grant a stay in exceptional circumstances.
translation: Filing an appeal automatically suspends an enforcement notice pending appeal resolution, unless the notice was issued with an urgency/immediate effect statement. The Tribunal may grant stays on application, and may discharge or stay urgent enforcement notices in exceptional circumstances.

title: Contempt of Tribunal
translation: The Tribunal has the same powers to punish contempt as a superior court. Contempt includes disobeying Tribunal orders, obstructing proceedings, and publishing material interfering with justice. Penalties are up to RM5,000 fine and/or 6 months imprisonment.

title: Disclosure of interest
verbatim:
(1) The President or a member of the Tribunal who has, in any capacity, a personal interest in any matter being heard by the Tribunal shall—
(a) disclose the nature of the interest to the parties; and
(b) not take part in the proceedings in respect of that matter, unless all parties consent to his participation.
(2) A member who fails to comply with this section commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding fifty thousand ringgit.
translation: Tribunal members must disclose personal interests in matters before them and must not participate in those proceedings without all parties' consent. Failure to disclose is a criminal offence.

title: Protection of members
verbatim: No action, suit, prosecution or other proceedings shall be brought, instituted or maintained in any court or tribunal against the President or any member of the Tribunal for or on account of or in respect of any act, matter or thing done or omitted to be done in good faith in the exercise or purported exercise of any power, or in the performance or purported performance of any function conferred upon the Tribunal by this Act.
translation: The President and members of the Appeal Tribunal enjoy statutory immunity from legal proceedings for acts done in good faith in the exercise of their statutory functions.

title: Procedure of Tribunal
verbatim:
(1) The Tribunal shall regulate its own procedure.
(2) Subject to any rules prescribed by the Minister, the Tribunal may adopt such procedure as it considers appropriate for the hearing and determination of appeals.
(3) The Tribunal is not bound by the rules of evidence.
(4) The Tribunal shall observe the rules of natural justice.
(5) The Tribunal may, unless the parties agree otherwise, require evidence to be given on oath or affirmation.
translation: The Tribunal has broad discretion to regulate its own procedures, is not bound by formal evidence rules, but must observe natural justice. Evidence may be required under oath or affirmation. The Minister may make regulations governing procedure.

title: Expert witnesses
verbatim:
(1) The Tribunal may require any person to give evidence at a hearing.
(2) A person summoned to give evidence before the Tribunal shall be paid the same expenses and fees as are payable to a witness in civil proceedings in the High Court.
(3) The Tribunal may appoint any person with relevant expertise as an expert witness to assist the Tribunal in dealing with technical matters.
translation: The Tribunal may require witnesses and may appoint expert witnesses for technical matters. Witnesses are entitled to the same expenses and fees as High Court witnesses in civil proceedings.

title: Costs
verbatim:
(1) The Tribunal may award costs in relation to an appeal.
(2) In determining costs, the Tribunal shall have regard to—
(a) the outcome of the appeal;
(b) the conduct of the parties; and
(c) any other relevant circumstances.
(3) Costs awarded by the Tribunal are enforceable as if they were orders of the High Court.
translation: The Tribunal may award costs in appeals, having regard to the outcome, conduct of parties, and other circumstances. Awarded costs are enforceable as High Court orders.

title: Enforcement of Tribunal's decision
verbatim:
(1) A decision of the Tribunal is binding on the parties.
(2) The Tribunal may enforce a decision by application to the High Court.
(3) The High Court may enforce the Tribunal's decision as if it were a decision of the High Court.
translation: Tribunal decisions are binding and may be enforced through the High Court, which treats them as its own orders for enforcement purposes.

title: Appeal from Tribunal to High Court
verbatim:
(1) Any party dissatisfied with a decision of the Tribunal on a question of law may appeal to the High Court within thirty days of the notification of the Tribunal's decision.
(2) An appeal to the High Court under this section shall be by way of case stated.
(3) The High Court's decision on the appeal shall be final.
translation: Parties may appeal Tribunal decisions to the High Court on questions of law within 30 days, by way of case stated. High Court decisions on such appeals are final.

title: Sittings of Tribunal
verbatim:
(1) The Tribunal may sit in divisions consisting of not less than three members, including the President.
(2) The President shall preside at every sitting of the Tribunal.
(3) In the absence of the President, a member designated by the Minister shall preside.
(4) The Tribunal may sit at any place in Malaysia.
translation: The Tribunal sits with at least 3 members including the President. In the President's absence, a designated member presides. The Tribunal may sit anywhere in Malaysia.

title: Power to make rules
translation: The Minister may make rules governing all aspects of Tribunal practice and procedure. Until such rules are made, the Tribunal may adopt its own appropriate procedures.

title: Inspection
translation: The Commissioner and authorised officers have broad inspection powers to enter premises where personal data is processed, inspect equipment, require document production, examine and copy records, and question staff. Reasonable notice must normally be given. Obstruction is a criminal offence.

title: Complaints
translation: Any person believing there has been a contravention may lodge a written complaint with the Commissioner using the prescribed form. The Commissioner must acknowledge within 14 days and may request additional information. The Commissioner may decline frivolous, out-of-jurisdiction, out-of-time, or better-resolved-elsewhere complaints, with written reasons.

title: Investigation
translation: The Commissioner may investigate compliance matters on the Commissioner's own initiative or in response to complaints. Investigation powers include requiring information, summoning persons, inspecting premises, and examining documents. Failure to cooperate and provision of false information are criminal offences.

title: Power to require attendance
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, by written notice, require any person to attend before the Commissioner at a specified time and place to answer questions or provide information relevant to an investigation.
