(2) A person who fails to comply with a notice under subsection (1) without reasonable excuse commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year or to both.
(3) A person required to attend under this section is entitled to be accompanied by an advocate and solicitor.
(4) A person shall not be required to answer questions or produce documents that would tend to incriminate that person unless the Commissioner provides a written guarantee that the information will not be used in criminal proceedings against the person.
translation: The Commissioner may require attendance for questioning by written notice. Failure to attend without reasonable excuse is an offence. Those summoned are entitled to legal representation. Self-incriminating information is protected unless the Commissioner provides a written immunity guarantee.

title: Outcome of investigation
verbatim:
(1) After completing an investigation, the Commissioner shall—
(a) notify the data user and any complainant of the outcome;
(b) where a contravention is found, take such action as the Commissioner considers appropriate, including issuing an enforcement notice, requiring the payment of a financial penalty, or referring the matter for prosecution;
(c) where no contravention is found, notify the data user and complainant accordingly.
(2) The Commissioner shall complete an investigation within a reasonable time and inform the parties of expected timelines.
(3) A complainant who is dissatisfied with the outcome of an investigation may request a review by the Commissioner.
(4) A data user who is dissatisfied with the outcome of an investigation may appeal to the Tribunal.
translation: After investigation, the Commissioner must notify all parties of the outcome. Where a contravention is found, the Commissioner may issue enforcement notices, require financial penalties, or refer for prosecution. Dissatisfied complainants may request review; dissatisfied data users may appeal to the Tribunal.

title: Mediation
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, with the consent of the parties, attempt to resolve a complaint through mediation before conducting a formal investigation.
(2) Anything said or written in the course of mediation is without prejudice and is not admissible in any subsequent proceedings.
(3) If mediation is unsuccessful, the Commissioner may proceed with a formal investigation.
translation: The Commissioner may attempt to resolve complaints through mediation with parties' consent. Statements made in mediation are without prejudice and inadmissible in subsequent proceedings. Failed mediation may proceed to formal investigation.

title: Power to issue notices in lieu of prosecution
verbatim:
(1) Where the Commissioner is satisfied that a data user has committed an offence under this Act and the circumstances are such that a formal prosecution is not warranted, the Commissioner may, instead of referring the matter for prosecution, issue a notice to the data user requiring the data user to pay a financial penalty not exceeding the amount that could have been imposed if the data user had been convicted of the offence.
(2) A data user who receives a notice under subsection (1) may, within thirty days, pay the financial penalty or appeal to the Tribunal against the notice.
(3) If the financial penalty is not paid and no appeal is made within the specified period, the Commissioner may refer the matter for prosecution.
(4) Payment of a financial penalty pursuant to a notice under this section shall not be treated as a conviction for any purpose.
translation: Where prosecution is not warranted, the Commissioner may issue a civil financial penalty notice instead. The data user has 30 days to pay or appeal. Unpaid penalties without appeal result in prosecution referral. Payment does not constitute a criminal conviction.

title: Assistance by police
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner or any authorised officer may call upon any police officer to provide assistance in the exercise of any power under this Part.
(2) A police officer providing assistance under this section shall have such powers as are necessary to render such assistance.
translation: The Commissioner and authorised officers may call upon police assistance for inspections and enforcement activities under this Part. Police officers assisting have the necessary powers to render that assistance.

title: Confidentiality of complaints
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner shall maintain the confidentiality of all complaints and investigations unless the complainant consents to disclosure or disclosure is required by law.
(2) The Commissioner shall not disclose the identity of a complainant to the data user being investigated without the complainant's consent, unless such disclosure is required for the investigation or proceedings.
(3) Any person who improperly discloses information obtained during a complaint or investigation commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit.
translation: Complaints and investigations are confidential. The Commissioner must not disclose the complainant's identity to the investigated data user without consent, unless necessary for the investigation. Improper disclosure is a criminal offence.

title: Appointment of enforcement officers
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may appoint any officer or class of officers as enforcement officers for the purposes of this Act.
(2) The Commissioner shall issue an enforcement officer with an identification card and a certificate of appointment.
(3) An enforcement officer shall, when exercising any power under this Act, produce the identification card and certificate of appointment upon request.
(4) A failure to produce the identification card or certificate of appointment does not make the enforcement officer's actions invalid if the officer is genuinely an enforcement officer.
translation: The Commissioner appoints enforcement officers who must carry and produce identification cards and certificates of appointment when exercising their powers. Failure to produce identification does not invalidate enforcement actions if the officer is genuinely authorised.

title: Power to enter and search
verbatim:
(1) An enforcement officer who has reasonable grounds to believe that an offence under this Act has been or is being committed may enter any premises to search for evidence.
(2) The enforcement officer may—
(a) search the premises and any persons found therein;
(b) examine any document, record, computer, equipment, or other material;
(c) seize any material that may be evidence of an offence;
(d) require any person to produce documents or to operate any computer or equipment.
(3) Entry and search under this section shall, in the first instance, be authorised by a warrant issued by a Magistrate.
(4) An enforcement officer may enter and search without a warrant if—
(a) it is not practicable to obtain a warrant; and
(b) there is reasonable belief that evidence may be lost or destroyed if a warrant is obtained first.
(5) A person who obstructs an enforcement officer in the exercise of powers under this section commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
translation: Enforcement officers may enter and search premises with a Magistrate's warrant where reasonable grounds exist to believe an offence has been or is being committed. Warrantless entry is permitted in urgent circumstances where evidence may be lost. Obstruction is a criminal offence.

title: Power to seize
verbatim:
(1) An enforcement officer may seize any article or material which the officer reasonably believes to be evidence of an offence under this Act.
(2) An enforcement officer may seize any computer or computer system, equipment, or storage medium if the officer has reasonable grounds to believe that it contains or has been used in connection with the commission of an offence.
(3) Seized items shall be detained and held in safe custody.
(4) A receipt shall be issued to the person from whom the items are seized.
(5) Seized items shall be returned to the owner if—
(a) no proceedings are commenced within six months of the seizure; or
(b) the owner is acquitted.
(6) Items may be retained beyond six months with the approval of a Magistrate.
translation: Enforcement officers may seize evidence, computers, and storage media connected to suspected offences. Seized items require a receipt to be issued and must be safely stored. Items must be returned if no prosecution commences within 6 months or if the owner is acquitted (unless a Magistrate approves extended retention).

title: Access to computers
verbatim:
(1) An enforcement officer who is empowered to enter premises and search may have access to any computer on the premises.
(2) The enforcement officer may—
(a) require any person having charge of, or otherwise concerned with the operation of, the computer to provide facilities to enable the officer to have access to and use the computer;
(b) require the person to provide the officer with any information necessary to access and use the computer, including passwords and encryption keys.
(3) A person who fails to comply with a requirement under subsection (2) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to both.
translation: Enforcement officers have the right to access computers on searched premises and may require persons to provide access, passwords, and encryption keys. Failure to provide computer access assistance is a criminal offence.

title: Production order
translation: A Magistrate may issue a production order requiring specified documents and records to be produced. The order must specify what is required, from whom, and when. Non-compliance is a criminal offence.

title: Power of arrest
verbatim:
(1) An enforcement officer may arrest without warrant any person who the officer has reasonable grounds to believe has committed or is committing an offence under this Act.
(2) An enforcement officer who arrests a person under this section shall—
(a) inform the arrested person of the grounds of the arrest;
(b) take the person before a Magistrate within twenty-four hours of the arrest, excluding the time taken to travel from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's court.
(3) An arrested person shall not be held in custody for more than twenty-four hours unless a Magistrate orders continued detention.
translation: Enforcement officers may arrest without warrant persons reasonably believed to have committed an offence. The arrested person must be informed of the reasons for arrest and brought before a Magistrate within 24 hours. Detention beyond 24 hours requires a Magistrate's order.

title: Obstruction of enforcement officers
translation: Obstruction, hindrance, failure to comply with lawful requirements, or assault and use of threatening language against enforcement officers are criminal offences punishable by up to RM300,000 fine and/or 2 years imprisonment.

title: Power to compound offences
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may compound any offence under this Act by accepting from any person reasonably suspected of having committed the offence a sum of money not exceeding fifty per centum of the maximum fine that may be imposed for that offence.
(2) On payment of the compounded sum, no further proceedings shall be taken against the person in respect of the offence.
(3) The Commissioner shall maintain records of all compounds accepted and such records shall be included in the annual report.
(4) Sums received as compounds shall be paid into the Fund.
translation: The Commissioner has power to compound (settle) offences by accepting up to 50% of the maximum fine for the offence. Payment ends all proceedings for that offence. Compounding records must be kept and included in the annual report. Compound sums go into the Fund.

title: Forfeiture
verbatim:
(1) The court before which a prosecution for an offence under this Act is heard may order the forfeiture of any article or material used in or in connection with the commission of the offence.
(2) Where a person is convicted of an offence under this Act, the court may, in addition to any other penalty, order the forfeiture of any article used in connection with the offence.
(3) Where no prosecution is commenced, or where a person charged with an offence is acquitted, seized items shall be returned to the owner.
translation: Courts may order forfeiture of items used in connection with an offence in addition to other penalties. Where no prosecution is commenced or the accused is acquitted, seized items are returned to the owner.

title: Liability of director, manager, secretary
verbatim:
(1) Where an offence under this Act is committed by a body corporate, any person who at the time of the commission of the offence was a director, manager, secretary, or other similar officer of the body corporate, or who was purporting to act in any such capacity, shall be guilty of that offence unless—
(a) the offence was committed without the person's consent or connivance; and
(b) the person had exercised all such diligence to prevent the commission of the offence as the person ought to have exercised, having regard to the nature of the person's functions and all the circumstances.
(2) A person convicted of an offence under subsection (1) shall be liable to the penalty prescribed for that offence.
translation: Corporate officers (directors, managers, secretaries) are personally liable for offences committed by the body corporate unless they prove the offence was committed without their consent or connivance and that they exercised all due diligence to prevent it.

title: Offences committed by partnership
verbatim:
Where an offence under this Act is committed by a partnership, a partner who—
(a) consented to or connived at the commission of the offence; or
(b) failed to exercise all diligence to prevent the commission of the offence,
shall be guilty of that offence and shall be liable to the penalty prescribed for that offence.
translation: Where a partnership commits an offence, individual partners who consented to, connived at, or failed to exercise due diligence to prevent the offence are personally liable.

title: Order for payment of financial penalty
translation: Following investigation, the Commissioner may impose financial penalties up to the prescribed maximum. Penalty amounts consider the severity of contravention, affected data subjects, remedial steps, financial capacity, prior violations, cooperation, and financial benefit from the breach. Data users may appeal to the Tribunal.

title: Recovery of financial penalties
verbatim:
(1) A financial penalty imposed by the Commissioner under this Act, if not paid within the period specified, may be recovered as a civil debt by the Commissioner.
(2) The Commissioner may apply to the court to have the financial penalty registered as a judgment debt, which shall then be enforceable accordingly.
(3) Interest shall accrue on unpaid financial penalties at the rate prescribed under any written law relating to judgment debts.
translation: Unpaid financial penalties are recoverable as civil debts. The Commissioner may register them as judgment debts with the court for enforcement. Interest accrues on unpaid penalties.

title: Rights of persons in investigations
translation: Persons under investigation have rights to know the nature of the investigation, make representations, have legal representation, and access evidence to be used against them (subject to public interest exceptions). The Commissioner is not required to identify complainants.

title: Preservation order
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may apply to a Magistrate for a preservation order in respect of any data that the Commissioner has reason to believe may be relevant to an investigation under this Act.
(2) A preservation order shall require the person having control of the data to preserve and maintain its integrity pending further investigation or proceedings.
(3) A preservation order shall be for a period not exceeding ninety days, subject to renewal.
(4) A person who fails to comply with a preservation order commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one hundred thousand ringgit or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year or to both.
translation: The Commissioner may apply to a Magistrate for a preservation order requiring specified data to be preserved intact for up to 90 days (renewable) pending investigation. Non-compliance is a criminal offence.

title: Evidence obtained by enforcement officers
verbatim:
(1) Evidence obtained by enforcement officers in the exercise of their powers under this Act shall be admissible in any proceedings under this Act.
(2) A document obtained or produced under this Act shall, unless the contrary is proved, be presumed to be authentic.
(3) In any proceedings, a certificate signed by the Commissioner stating that a document is a true copy of an original document held by the Commissioner shall be conclusive evidence of the contents of the original document.
translation: Evidence obtained by enforcement officers is admissible in proceedings. Documents are presumed authentic unless the contrary is proved. Commissioner-certified copies are conclusive evidence of original document contents.

title: Jurisdiction of courts
verbatim:
(1) A Sessions Court shall have jurisdiction to try any offence under this Act and to impose the full penalty prescribed therefor, notwithstanding anything in the Criminal Procedure Code [Act 593].
(2) Notwithstanding subsection (1), the Public Prosecutor may, having regard to the nature and gravity of the offence, direct that an offence under this Act be tried in the High Court.
translation: Sessions Courts have full jurisdiction to try all offences under the Act and impose maximum penalties, notwithstanding the Criminal Procedure Code. The Public Prosecutor may direct that serious offences be tried in the High Court.

title: Public prosecutor
verbatim:
(1) Prosecutions for offences under this Act shall not be instituted except with the consent of the Public Prosecutor.
(2) Notwithstanding subsection (1), the Commissioner may, with the written authority of the Public Prosecutor, institute prosecutions for offences under this Act.
translation: All prosecutions under the Act require the Public Prosecutor's consent. However, the Commissioner may institute prosecutions directly if granted written authority by the Public Prosecutor.

title: General offences
verbatim:
(1) A data user who contravenes any of the personal data protection principles commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or
(c) to both.
(2) A data user who processes personal data in contravention of section 13 (processing without registration) commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or
(c) to both.
(3) Where a person is convicted of a continuing offence under this Act, the court may, in addition to any penalty imposed for the initial offence, impose a further fine not exceeding one thousand ringgit for each day or part of a day during which the offence continued after conviction.
translation: Contravening any of the seven data protection principles or processing without registration are the primary offences under the Act. Enhanced penalties of up to RM500,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment apply following the 2024 Amendment. Continuing offences attract an additional RM1,000 per day fine after conviction.

title: Offences in relation to personal data
verbatim:
(1) A person who obtains, discloses, or procures the disclosure of personal data without the consent of the data user commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or
(c) to both.
(2) A person who sells personal data obtained in contravention of subsection (1) commits an offence.
(3) A person who offers for sale personal data which has been obtained in contravention of subsection (1) commits an offence.
(4) A person convicted of an offence under subsection (2) or (3) shall be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding five hundred thousand ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or
(c) to both.
(5) It is a defence for a person charged with an offence under this section to prove that—
(a) obtaining, disclosing or procuring the disclosure was necessary for preventing or detecting crime;
(b) the obtaining, disclosing or procuring was required or authorized by or under any law or by any court; or
(c) the person acted in the reasonable belief that the person had in law a right to obtain, disclose or procure the disclosure.
translation: It is an offence to obtain, disclose, or procure disclosure of personal data without the data user's consent, and to sell or offer to sell such unlawfully obtained data. Penalties are up to RM500,000 and/or 3 years imprisonment (as enhanced by 2024 Amendment). Defences include crime prevention, legal authority, and reasonable belief of lawful right.

title: Cross-border transfer of personal data
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment (Act A1699), this section comprehensively governs cross-border personal data transfers. Transfers are only permitted to adequacy-designated countries, or under specific safeguards: data subject consent, contract necessity, public interest, legal proceedings, vital interests, public register data, Commissioner-approved standard contractual clauses (SCCs), binding corporate rules (BCRs), approved certification schemes, or international agreements. Unlawful transfers carry penalties of up to RM500,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment.

title: Data protection officer
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment (Act A1699), prescribed classes of data users must appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO). The DPO's functions include advising on compliance, monitoring the Act's application, cooperating with the Commissioner, and acting as data subject contact point. The DPO must have adequate resources, data access, and independence from conflicts of interest. The DPO may be internal or external. Failure to appoint is an offence punishable by up to RM250,000 fine and/or 2 years imprisonment.

title: Liability of body corporate
verbatim:
(1) Where a body corporate is convicted of an offence under this Act, the court may, in lieu of or in addition to any fine, order the body corporate to pay a financial penalty not exceeding the greater of—
(a) three million ringgit; or
(b) ten per centum of the annual turnover of the body corporate in Malaysia for the preceding year.
(2) For the purposes of this section, 'annual turnover' means the total revenue of the body corporate from all commercial activities in Malaysia during the relevant year.
translation: Enhanced penalties for corporate bodies: courts may impose up to RM3 million or 10% of annual Malaysian turnover (whichever is greater) on top of or instead of other fines. This provision targets large organizations by linking penalties to business scale.

title: Service of notices
translation: Documents under the Act may be served personally, left at the last known address, sent by registered post (deemed delivered in ordinary course of post), or sent electronically to the registered email address (deemed served on successful transmission).

title: Evidence
verbatim:
(1) In any proceedings under this Act, a certificate purporting to be signed by the Commissioner stating any fact shall be admissible as evidence of the fact stated.
(2) In any proceedings for an offence under this Act, it shall be sufficient to state in the charge the substance of the offence without setting out the full details.
(3) A document produced from a computer system shall be admissible as evidence if—
(a) the computer system was operating properly at the material time; and
(b) a certificate to that effect has been given by a competent person.
translation: Commissioner-signed certificates are admissible as evidence of their contents. Charges need only state the substance of the offence. Computer-generated documents are admissible with a certificate of proper system operation.

title: Presumptions
verbatim:
(1) For the purposes of this Act, where personal data is found in the possession of a person, that person shall be presumed, until the contrary is proved, to have processed such personal data.
(2) Where personal data found in a person's possession contains information relating to identifiable individuals, the personal data shall be presumed, until the contrary is proved, to have been processed in contravention of this Act if the person is not a registered data user in respect of that class of data and has not established any lawful basis for processing.
translation: Statutory presumptions apply: a person in possession of personal data is presumed to have processed it. A non-registered person in possession of personal data is presumed to have processed it unlawfully. Both presumptions are rebuttable by the accused.

title: Liability of officers of body corporate
verbatim:
(1) Where a body corporate commits an offence under this Act, any person who at the time of the commission of the offence was a director, chief executive officer, chief operating officer, manager, secretary, or other similar officer of the body corporate, or was purporting to act in any such capacity, or was in any manner responsible for the management of any of the affairs of the body corporate, shall also be guilty of that offence and shall be liable to the same penalty as the body corporate if it is proved that—
(a) the offence was committed with the consent or connivance of that person; or
(b) the commission of the offence was attributable to any neglect on the part of that person.
(2) A person may be proceeded against and convicted under this section notwithstanding that the body corporate has not been convicted of the offence.
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment (Act A1699), this section pierces the corporate veil to hold individual officers personally liable for PDPA offences committed by a body corporate. Directors, CEOs, COOs, managers, secretaries, and similar officers face the same penalties as the body corporate where the offence was committed with their consent, connivance, or due to their neglect. Officers can be prosecuted even if the body corporate itself has not been convicted.

title: Public servants
verbatim: For the purposes of the Penal Code [Act 574], the Commissioner, every member of the Advisory Committee and the Appeal Tribunal, and every officer, employee, and agent of the Commissioner acting in the performance of his duty under this Act shall be deemed to be public servants.
translation: The Commissioner, Advisory Committee and Tribunal members, and all Commissioner staff acting in official capacity are deemed public servants under the Penal Code. This attracts additional protections and obligations applicable to public servants.

title: Power to make regulations
translation: The Minister has broad power to make regulations covering prescribed classes of data users, forms, fees, consent mechanisms, data breach notification procedures, DPO requirements, data protection impact assessments, and other implementing details. Regulations may include offences with penalties within the Act's limits.

title: Unauthorized sale of personal data
verbatim:
(1) Any person who sells, offers to sell, or procures the sale of personal data obtained or collected in contravention of this Act commits an offence.
(2) Any person who purchases personal data knowing or having reasonable grounds to believe that the personal data has been obtained or collected in contravention of this Act commits an offence.
(3) A person convicted of an offence under this section shall be liable—
(a) to a fine not exceeding one million ringgit;
(b) to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years; or
(c) to both.
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment (Act A1699), this section creates specific offences targeting the unauthorized commercial trade in personal data. It is an offence to sell, offer to sell, or procure the sale of unlawfully obtained personal data. It is also an offence to knowingly purchase such data. Penalties are among the highest in the Act: up to RM1,000,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment.

title: Data protection officer
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment, prescribed classes of data users must appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO). The DPO's functions include advising on compliance, monitoring the Act's application, cooperating with the Commissioner, and acting as data subject contact point. The DPO must have adequate resources, data access, and independence from conflicts of interest. The DPO may be internal or external, and details must be registered with the Commissioner and published for data subjects. Failure to appoint is an offence.

title: Data protection impact assessment
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment, data users must conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) before high-risk processing activities. High-risk indicators include new technologies, large-scale processing, and sensitive data. Where high risks cannot be mitigated, prior consultation with the Commissioner is required. DPIAs must document processing description, necessity, risk assessment, and mitigating measures. Failure to conduct a required DPIA is an offence.

title: Records of processing activities
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment, prescribed class data users must maintain records of all processing activities (a data processing register or RoPA). Records must include: data user and DPO details, processing purposes, data categories, recipients, cross-border transfers and safeguards, retention periods, and security measures. Records must be made available to the Commissioner on request. Failure to maintain records is an offence.

title: Codes of practice
translation: The Commissioner issues industry-specific codes of practice following public consultation (at least 30 days), Advisory Committee review, and Ministerial approval. Codes of practice are admissible as evidence in proceedings and are taken into account by courts and tribunals.

title: Amendment of Schedule
verbatim: The Minister may by order amend any Schedule to this Act.
translation: The Minister has broad power to amend any Schedule to the Act by order, allowing flexibility to update prescribed classes of data users, sensitive personal data categories, and other scheduled matters without requiring primary legislation.

title: Power to issue directions
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may, with the approval of the Minister, issue general directions to data users concerning the processing of personal data.
(2) General directions issued under this section shall be published in the Gazette.
(3) A data user who fails to comply with a general direction commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding three hundred thousand ringgit.
translation: The Commissioner may issue general directions to data users (with Ministerial approval), published in the Gazette. Non-compliance with directions is a criminal offence punishable by up to RM300,000 fine.

title: Correction of errors in decisions
verbatim:
(1) The Commissioner may correct any clerical mistake or error arising from an accidental slip or omission in any decision or notice issued under this Act.
(2) The correction shall be notified to all parties affected by the decision or notice.
translation: The Commissioner may correct clerical errors, slips, or omissions in decisions and notices and must notify all affected parties of such corrections.

title: Repeal
verbatim: Any written law relating to personal data protection in force immediately before the commencement of this Act is hereby repealed to the extent that it is inconsistent with this Act.
translation: Any existing laws relating to personal data protection that are inconsistent with this Act are repealed to the extent of the inconsistency upon the Act's commencement.

title: Mandatory data breach notification
translation: Inserted by the 2024 Amendment (Act A1699), this is the cornerstone new provision introducing mandatory data breach notification in Malaysia. Data users must notify the Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of a data breach likely to cause significant harm. Simultaneously (or as soon as practicable), affected data subjects must also be notified. The Commissioner notification must include: breach description, data categories and volume, DPO contact details, likely consequences, and remedial measures. Phased notifications are permitted where full information is unavailable. Data subjects must receive a plain-language notification including consequences and self-protection recommendations. Notification to data subjects may be delayed if it would impede a criminal investigation. All breaches (notified or not) must be recorded. Failure to comply is an offence with penalties up to RM500,000 and/or 3 years imprisonment.

title: Minister's power to exempt
translation: The Minister has broad power to grant conditional or unconditional exemptions from any provisions of the Act by Gazette notification. In considering exemptions, the Minister must assess the nature of data, processing purposes, potential harm to data subjects, and the public interest.

title: Savings
translation: Savings provisions protect existing rights, obligations, legal proceedings, and investigations that pre-date the Act's commencement. Prior proceedings continue under applicable prior law. Existing appeal rights are preserved.

title: Transitional provisions
verbatim:
(1) Any person who is processing personal data immediately before the commencement of this Act and is required to be registered as a data user under this Act shall apply for registration within such period as may be prescribed by the Minister.
(2) A person referred to in subsection (1) who is processing personal data in good faith in reliance on the applicable law immediately before the commencement of this Act may continue to do so until—
(a) the expiry of the period prescribed by the Minister under subsection (1); or
(b) the determination of the person's application for registration, whichever is the later.
(3) Following the commencement of the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024—
(a) a data user that was registered before the Amendment Act came into force shall be deemed to be registered in accordance with the requirements of the amended Act for a period of twelve months from the commencement of the Amendment Act, or until the data user's registration falls due for renewal, whichever is the earlier;
(b) a data user that is required to appoint a data protection officer shall appoint a data protection officer within twelve months of the commencement of the relevant provision;
(c) a data user that is required to maintain records of processing activities shall comply with that requirement within twelve months of the commencement of the relevant provision;
(d) a data user that is required to carry out a data protection impact assessment in respect of existing processing activities shall do so within eighteen months of the commencement of the relevant provision.
(4) Any code of practice in force immediately before the commencement of the Amendment Act shall continue in force until it is replaced or revoked by a new code of practice.
(5) The Commissioner shall, within six months of the commencement of the Amendment Act, publish guidance on compliance with the new requirements introduced by the Amendment Act.
translation: Transitional provisions govern the orderly implementation of both the original Act and the 2024 Amendment. Existing processors must register within the prescribed period and may continue operating during that period. Following the 2024 Amendment: existing registrations are deemed compliant for 12 months; DPO appointments must be made within 12 months; records of processing must be established within 12 months; DPIAs for existing processing must be completed within 18 months. Existing codes of practice remain in force. The Commissioner must publish compliance guidance within 6 months of the Amendment's commencement.
